summary: This source outlines Socrates' philosophical approach, primarily his use of the elenchus (question and answer) and his self-described role as a 'midwife' of ideas. It also highlights his ethical stance against unbridled hedonism, specifically his insistence that pleasure is not equivalent to the good.

principles.statement: Pleasure is not the equivalent of the good.
principles.rationale: Socrates is suspicious of the view that pleasure is intrinsically worthy and criticizes unbridled hedonism.
principles.source_quote: pleasure is not the equivalent of the good

frameworks.name: Elenchus (Socratic Method)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When conducting philosophical activity and examining ideas.
steps: Conduct inquiry by means of question and answer.
Embrace the practice of collection and division.
Proceed from hypotheses to first principles.
frameworks.source_quote: Socrates conducted his philosophical activity by means of question an answer, and we typically associate with him a method called the elenchus .

mental_models.name: The Philosophical Midwife
mental_models.description: A metaphor for a philosopher who claims to have no ideas of their own, but instead uses dialectic questioning to help others give birth to their own ideas.

signature_quotes.text: ...who has no ideas of his own but helps give birth to the ideas of others...
signature_quotes.context: Describing his role as a philosophical midwife.

signature_quotes.text: pleasure is not the equivalent of the good
signature_quotes.context: Socrates' argument against hedonism in Plato's Gorgias.

anti_patterns.description: Embracing unbridled hedonism and viewing pleasure as intrinsically worthy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Pleasure is not the equivalent of the good.
